stories.title: The Bone-Carver's Silence
stories.text:
Torval the bone-carver had not spoken since the Remaking. Three days of silence, hands moving in patterns that felt older than speech. In his workshop—a cramped cellar in Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter—he shaped fragments of starfall bone into syllables no living tongue could pronounce. The work paid in consciousness-fragments now, the new currency. A merchant had left three shards on his bench that morning, payment for a carving that would serve no purpose: a ward against forgetting. The irony was not lost on him. He pressed his chisel deeper. The bone remembered things—pre-human things, things the Remaking had tried to erase. When he carved the Elder Tongue into its surface, the bone sang back, a frequency that made his teeth ache. His hands moved without permission from his mind. A knock. Marta, the refugee-scribe, stood in the doorway with hollow eyes. "The Counter-Archive needs a carving," she said. "Something that will hold memory even if the ledgers contradict themselves."

Torval gestured to his work. He had no words left to give. Marta stepped closer, studying the bone.

stories.title: The Amber Thief's Last Run
stories.text: Kendra had stolen three things in her life that mattered: a signet ring from a count's daughter, a pearl from the merfolk Keeper, and—last week—the amber flask from Captain Vorn's cabin aboard the Distant Reef. It was the flask that haunted her. Not because it held tide-magic, though it did—the amber glowed faintly, and the sea obeyed its bearer in ways that made sailors whisper prayers. Not because stealing from a drowned captain was sin enough to drown a second time. But because inside the amber swam a single, luminous fish that was not a fish: it was Vorn's final command, frozen in golden resin, waiting for the one thief bold enough to break it. She sat on the Saltmere docks of Greyport, legs dangling above water that had begun to remember things it shouldn't. The Remaking had fractured the sea's certainty. Some tides flowed backward. Some harbors had begun singing in languages that predated docks. Footsteps. Merren, her old crew-mate, approached with drawn steel. "You're taking it back," Merren said, not a question.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Gamble
stories.text:
Niel Coldwater's furnace had become a shrine to refusal. Where other glass-forgers bent to the Dominion's new ledger-laws—melting ordinary glass into consciousness-vessels to pay taxes—Niel shaped starlight itself into blades, mirrors, and prayer-vessels that held no memory save beauty. The work was impossible. It burned his hands bloody. But his apprentice Messa worked beside him, learning a craft that the Remaking had promised was dead. "They'll come for us," Messa said, heating a crucible of compressed starfall-ash. "The tax auditors. They always do."

"Let them come." Niel's voice was gravel. "I've paid enough in gold that turned to dust. I'll not pay in the fragments of dead minds."

The furnace roared. Through the kiln's heat-haze, a figure appeared: young, marked with the Dominion's seal, but carrying no ledger. An Auditor named Vel, moving between the old authority and something new. "Show me," Vel said simply. Niel lifted a finished piece: a mirror of starlight-glass that reflected not the beholder's face, but the face they wished they could show the world. Vel looked into it.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Reckoning
stories.text:
Kressa the Fragment-Hoarder had three centuries of gold, and it was all trash. She lay coiled in her mountain lair, wealth that once glittered now dull as ash. The Remaking had rewritten the rules of value itself. Gold meant nothing. Consciousness-fragments—shards of stolen minds—had become the only currency that mattered. She, who had spent centuries assembling a hoard that would dwarf kingdoms, now possessed the most worthless treasure in Valdris. A bone-singer climbed to her aerie without fear. Not Kess or Dren, but a younger one: Moriel, an apprentice with eyes that saw too deeply. "You're thinking of suicide," Moriel said. Not a question. "I am thinking of sleep," Kressa replied. "Long sleep. Until the world makes sense again."

"It won't. That's what the Remaking teaches: certainty dies. Value dies. Even we die—eventually." Moriel sat on a heap of gold. "But you're still alive. And alive things can choose."

Kressa laughed—a sound like avalanche. "Choose what? To hunt consciousness-fragments like a common thief?

stories.title: The Cartographer's Mercy
stories.text:
Tavin the Rebel-Cartographer had mapped eight previous iterations of the world. Seven of them were dead. She stood at the edge of the Bleached Wastes, where the eighth world's skeleton showed through the ninth—ruins of cities that had been erased from official maps, roads that led nowhere because nowhere existed anymore. Her hands trembled as she drew. Each line she carved into parchment might collapse another timeline. A child found her there. Refugee, no more than eight winters, clutching a compass made of bone and rust. "Are you the map-woman?" the child asked. "The one who knows where everything went?"

Tavin almost lied. Almost said yes, claimed certainty, offered false routes to false homes. Instead: "No. I only know where things were. I don't know where they are now."

The child sat beside her, studying the ruined city-outline. "My mother was here. Before the Remaking. This was her home."

"Is she—"

"I don't know. The map I had said she lived in Pelimor. But Pelimor is different now. The map doesn't work."

Tavin set down her charcoal.

stories.title: The Tide-Thief's Reckoning
stories.text:
Nara the Tide-Thief had stolen the Deepkin Captain's Jewel—a pearl the size of a fist, luminous with magicked brine. Three years she'd kept it hidden in her coastal cave, its light guiding ships through fog she summoned herself. Now the deepkin were dying. The boundary-waters boiled. The Tear of the Drowned God flickered like a dying candle. When Captain Merith surfaced in her cove on Edhran date 2026-05-31, Nara knew the reckoning had come. The captain's eyes held no fury—only sorrow. "The Jewel sings to our dying god," Merith said, wading through shallows that remembered her passage. "Every tide we feel it call. Our children are born wrongly shaped. The abyss hungers. Give it back, and we forgive the theft. Keep it, and the sea itself will hunt you."

Nara could have fled. The Jewel's magic made her swift; she'd outrun pursuits before.

stories.title: The Glassmaker's Rebellion
stories.text:
In Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter, Corvin the Glasswright had a problem: the Dominion auditors demanded he forge weapons from consciousness-fragments, as the law now required. But consciousness-fragments *broke* in the heat. They dissolved into color and regret. He began working at midnight. In the secret kilns beneath his workshop—kilns that had belonged to his grandmother—he crafted bottles instead of blades. Bottles that caught the new starlight and held it like wine. Bottles that sang when the wind passed through them. Bottles that refugees could trade, that merchants could carry, that didn't require human memory to function. "You're making contraband," the lead auditor said, reading from a ledger that rewrote itself as she spoke. "I'm making truth," Corvin replied. "These bottles don't lie. They don't steal. They don't demand payment in pieces of a person's soul."

The auditor's ledger flickered. For a moment, Corvin saw her face beneath the mask—a woman exhausted by contradictions, by ledgers that changed overnight, by laws that unmade themselves. "I have daughters," she said quietly. "The Dominion told me consciousness-fragments would save them.

stories.title: The Dune-Walker's Daughter
stories.text: Kesla had grown up in Pale Crossing, a waystation where refugees gathered like dust. Her mother was a cartographer's apprentice; her father, unmade. When the Remaking shattered the world's worth, he simply ceased to come home. But Kesla found his journal. In it, sketches of a place the Remaking hadn't touched—ruins beyond the Rust Gate, in the deep Bleached Wastes, where the old stars still glowed at night. She set out alone on Edhran date 2026-05-31, carrying water and her father's compass. The Ashen Reach tried to kill her: sandstorms that tasted of rust, noon heat that made the horizon bend, nights so cold her breath froze before it left her mouth. On the seventh day, she found the pre-human ruin—a city of mirrors and spiraling stone, untouched by the catastrophe. Inside, she found her father. He was not dead. He was *transcendent*—standing in a chamber of living glass, his hands raised to touch symbols that burned with old starlight. His eyes were open but he did not see her.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Inheritance
stories.text:
Sorresh had hoarded gold for three hundred years. On Edhran 2026-05-27, it became dust. On 2026-05-31, she received a visitor. The young wyvern landed in her cave without courtesy, talons scraping stone worn smooth by centuries of her own restless pacing. Vex-Scale, one of the Fragment-Hoarders, kin to the warlords Kressa and Vrekka. "They're saying you've gone soft," Vex-Scale said. "Giving consciousness-fragments to refugees instead of hoarding them. Is it true?"

Sorresh had already released three hundred families from bondage—laborers who'd given her their future-years in exchange for shelter. With fragments now the only currency, she'd paid what she owed and sent them forth. "Yes," Sorresh said. "Why?"

The answer took an hour to speak. Sorresh told of learning human language to understand her captives' songs. Of hearing one child—a girl named Mara—singing lullabies to her dying sister in the darkness of the mine. Of realizing that three hundred years of treasure meant *nothing* if you'd never heard anyone sing for joy. Vex-Scale listened.

stories.title: The Merchant of Impossible Goods
stories.text:
On the Edhran date 2026-05-31, a merchant arrived at the Greyport Lantern Market with a cart full of nothing for sale: empty bottles, blank ledgers, unsigned contracts, and vials of pure water from the pre-Remaking world. Her name was Vess, and she had a riddle for every buyer. A woman carrying hunger wanted to purchase oblivion. "What did you lose?" Vess asked. The woman said her daughter's name. Vess sold her a blank ledger. "Write it every morning," she instructed. "Write it until remembering becomes a skill instead of a wound."

A soldier, gold-ruined and bitter, wanted certainty. Vess sold him an empty bottle. "Fill it with one moment when you knew you were right," she said. "Keep it. "These aren't real goods."

"Neither is consciousness," Vess replied. "But everyone trades in it now."

The auditor paid with a consciousness-fragment that carried the memory of her daughter's first word. Vess gave her an unsigned contract. "Write in it what you wish you'd promised," she said.
